**Quickly rendering a triangle in OpenGL**

[**https://github.com/kerplamp/OpenGLPresentationCode**](https://github.com/kerplamp/OpenGLPresentationCode)  
---

**Part 1: Downloading Visual Studio**

**Go to: [https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/downloads/](https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/downloads/)** 

**Download the community edition**

**Once it pulls up a window where you can choose what workloads you want, make sure you select ‘Desktop development  C++’**

**Click install**

**Continue with installer until you can create new project**

**Select “Console App” from the project templates**

---

**Part 2: Downloading GLAD and GLFW**  
	  
	**	Go to: [https://glad.dav1d.de/](https://glad.dav1d.de/)**

**Under API, there is a dropdown named ‘gl’**  
**Select 3.3 from that dropdown.**

**Under Profile, select ‘Core’**  
	  
			

| ![Image depicting what settings on the GLAD website you should select. There are only 2 settings that need to be changed. Use the dropbox with "gl" next to it to select "Version 3.3".Next use the dropbox under "Profile" to select "Core"][image1] |
| :---- |

	**Next, click ‘Generate’ in the bottom right**

	**Go to: [https://www.glfw.org/download](https://www.glfw.org/download)**

**Click the big orange button that says:**   
**‘64-bit Windows Binaries’**

---

**Part 3: Getting files ready**

**After you have everything downloaded, create an empty folder somewhere to keep all of the files we need for ease of access.**

**Within glfw-3.4.bin.WIN64:**  
**\-Move the ‘include’ folder into the folder you have**  
**created**  
**\-Move the ‘lib-vc2019’ folder into the folder you have created**

**Within glad.zip:**  
**\-Move the ‘include’ folder into the folder you have created**  
	**\-Move the ‘src’ folder into the folder you have created**

**Your folder should now look something like this**

| ![Picture of whats inside the openglspeedrun folder: 3 folders named include, lib-vc2019 and src.][image2] |
| :---- |

---

**Part 4: Setting up Visual Studio with GLAD and GLFW**

**On the right of your screen you should see a list of files.**

    

| ![Images showing that you must right click on your visual studio solution in the right hand menu. It will be named the same thing that you have named your project.Next you must click properties within the dropdown that has opened upon rightclicking.Within the properties window that is now open, we must go to the C/C++ sub-dropdown and under it select General.At the top of the menu that opens on the right side of the Properties window there will be "Additional Include Directories." Click on the entry and then click the dropdown arrow on the right, selecting "\<Edit...\>" afterwards.In the edit window, you must click the New Line button (it is a little folder with a yellow asterisk to the top left of it).Once we have a new line, click the ellipsis button and select the include folder within the file we have created.][image3] |
| :---- |

**You will not have glad.c at first, we will add this in a moment.**   
**Also your .cpp file may be named something else.**

		**Right click on the name of your Project and click ‘Properties’**  
		**(My project is named OpenGlLearning)**

		**Next you will need to do the following:**

			**Within the ‘C/C++’ submenu:**  
**\-Select ‘General’**  
**\-Select the little dropdown arrow for**  
**‘Addtional Include Directories’ on the right and click ‘\<Edit…\>’**

**Within the menu that appears upon clicking ‘\<Edit..\>’**  
**\-Click the yellow folder Icon with the orange star**  
**\-Click the ‘...’ that has appeared in the box below.**  
**\-Navigate to the folder you created earlier and click on the ‘include’ folder (only select it don’t open it)**  
**\-Click ‘Select Folder’ in the bottom right of the file explorer window**

			

| ![Image with visual instructions. Refer to the first image on this slide's alt text for textual instructions.][image4] |
| :---- |

**Within the ‘Linker’ submenu:**  
**\-Select ‘General’**  
**\-Select the little dropdown arrow for**  
**‘Addtional Dependencies’ on the right and click ‘\<Edit…\>’**  
**\-Manually copy the path to your glfw.lib folder into the top box**  
				  
				

| ![Image with visual instructions. Refer to the first image on this slide's alt text for textual instructions.][image5] |
| :---- |

**Within the folder you have created:**  
**\-Drag the glad.c file into the Source Files folder in Visual Studio**

| ![image depicting dragging the glad.c file into the Source Files folder in visual studio's explorer][image6] |
| :---- |

---

**Part 5: Drawing a Window**

	**Reminder that github is linked at top of doc with all code.**

	

| ![Image with code:\#include \<glad/glad.h\>\#include \<GLFW/glfw3.h\>int main(){&#9;glfwInit(); // initialize glfw, so we can use it to create a window and handle input     GLFWwindow\* window = glfwCreateWindow(800, 600, "OpenGL in 10 Minutes", NULL, NULL);&#9;// make a window with width 800, height 600, title "OpenGL in 10 Minutes"    glfwMakeContextCurrent(window);&#9;//what window are we going to write to? the one we just made.    gladLoadGLLoader((GLADloadproc)glfwGetProcAddress); // tell glad to get all our functions ready&#9;//while the window shouldnt close, keep doing this loop.    while (\!glfwWindowShouldClose(window))    {        //swap our buffers to update        glfwSwapBuffers(window);        glfwPollEvents();    }&#9;//all done, close the window and end the program    glfwTerminate();    return 0;}][image7] |
| :---- |

	**glfwInit():**  
**Initialize GLFW so we can use whatever operating system were on’s native functions through GLFW**

	**GLFWwindow\* window \= glfwCreateWindow(800, 600, “OpenGL in 10 Minutes…):**  
**Through GLFW, Create a window to use** 

	**glfwMakeContextCurrent(window):**  
**Use the window we just created, set it to current context so glad knows what window were using when we load it**

	**gladLoadGLLoader((GLADloadproc)glfwGetProcAddress):**  
**Load glad and get all our glad functions ready. This will let us use our OS’s openGL implementation.**

	**while(\!glfwWindowShouldClose(window)):**  
**Loop to run while window is open**

	**glfwSwapBuffers(window):**  
		**Update whats on the window**

	**glfwPollEvents():**  
		**Get user input such as closing the window or moving/resizing it**

---

**Part 6: Vertex Buffer Objects**

**Vertex buffer objects are practically long (or short) lists of data stored on the GPU rather than RAM to make them faster.**

**To use one we must first create a place to store its ID so that we can reference where it is and access it, remember this is only the ID of the VBO, not the actual VBO: unsigned int VBO;**

**Next we generate the buffer and use pass by reference to write the newly created VBO into the ID we just made: glGenBuffers(1, \&VBO);**

**Since we only want 1, we specify 1\.**

**Now we can access the VBO from its ID using OpenGL functions.**

	

| ![Image with code: unsigned int VBO; // blank sheet of paper glGenBuffers(1, \&VBO); // OpenGL, make a blank form to use on this paper //points of a triangle, just data for later float vertices\[\] = { -0.5f, -0.5f, 0.0f,  0.5f, -0.5f, 0.0f,  0.0f,  0.5f, 0.0f }; //handing OpenGL the VBO form, and telling it what kind of form glBindBuffer(GL\_ARRAY\_BUFFER, VBO); //okay now write to the form i gave you glBufferData(GL\_ARRAY\_BUFFER, sizeof(float) \* 9, vertices, GL\_STATIC\_DRAW); //           ^ type of buffer, ^ size of data,   ^ what data, ^ how often is it changed //                                (9 floats)                     (STATIC cuz no change)][image8] |
| :---- |

	  
**float vertices\[\] { … };**  
**Obviously we’ll need data to put into the VBO, so we create some.**

**glBindBuffer(GL\_ARRAY\_BUFFER, VBO):**  
**Now we need to actually put this data into the VBO. To do this we must first ‘bind’ the VBO.**

**glBufferData(GL\_ARRAY\_BUFFER, sizeof(float)\*9, vertices, GL\_STATIC\_DRAW);**  
	**Now we can interact with our *bound* VBO through OpenGL functions like this.**

**We tell it is a GL\_ARRAY\_BUFFER, the size of the data and where its coming from. Since we hard coded the vertices and they aren’t going to change, we use GL\_STATIC\_DRAW.**

**If you’ll notice, we don't tell OpenGL to use our ‘VBO’ variable within glBufferData. We only tell it to use GL\_ARRAY\_BUFFER. Which is what we have bound our VBO as.**

**Now we have all our data about our triangle in our VBO, OpenGL doesn't quite know how to read this data yet though, as we can store all sorts of data in VBOs.** 

**Things like color of the vertex and texture coordinates are often stored alongside positional data in VBOs. But for now we are simply keeping the coordinates of each vertex of the triangle, without any other data.**

---

**Part 6: Shaders**

**Shaders are what help OpenGL know what to do with the data we give it through the VBO**

**First is the Vertex Shader.**

**The vertex shader is practically what moves and transforms our objects/vertices.**

**We have put the ‘shape’ of our triangle into the VBO, the Vertex Shader will be what we use to move the shape around, or stretch/squash/etc it.**

		  
			

| ![Image with code: const char\* vertexShaderSource = "\#version 330 core\\n" // our shader code, 1 big string     "layout (location = 0) in vec3 aPos;\\n"     "void main()\\n"     "{\\n"     "   gl\_Position = vec4(aPos.x, aPos.y, aPos.z, 1.0);\\n"     "}\\0"; unsigned int vertexShader; // blank sheet of paper vertexShader = glCreateShader(GL\_VERTEX\_SHADER); // make a blank shader form // FOR SOME REASON THIS ONE WE JUST SET EQUAL //              V form       V how many strings  V where the code is  glShaderSource(vertexShader, 1, \&vertexShaderSource, NULL); //what do we fill the form with glCompileShader(vertexShader); // okay we've given you all the info, now sign the form][image9] |
| :---- |

**Above is shader code defined in the vertexShaderSource variable as a C-string.**

**We tell it what version we are using and what profile with \#version 330 core.**

**Layout (location \= 0\) in vec3 aPos;**  
**This is essentially a parameter for our shader code like how we would use in a function, except these are just read from the VBO. This will come up later.**

	**gl\_Position \= vec4(aPos.x, aPos.y, aPos.z, 1.0)**  
**Here we set a variable inherit to OpenGL called gl\_Position. This is how we tell OpenGL where we want the position to be. We can do manipulations to the vec4 we create to modify the position, like mentioned above.** 

**Something like vec4(aPos.x, aPos.y, aPos.z, 1.0) \* 2 would scale all our positions by 2, thus doubling the size of our triangle.**

**The rest of the code is fairly self explanatory through the comments. We create a vertexShader ID and use 	glCreateShader(GL\_VERTEX\_SHADER) to lock it in as a vertex shader. Afterwards we set the source and compile the vertex shader.**

**Next is the Fragment Shader**

**The fragment shader is what we use to tell OpenGL what color to use.**

| ![Image with code:const char\* fragmentShaderSource = "\#version 330 core\\n" // our shader code, 1 big string, again    "out vec4 FragColor;\\n"    "void main()\\n"    "{\\n"    "   FragColor = vec4(1.0f, 0.5f, 0.2f, 1.0f);\\n"    "}\\n\\0";unsigned int fragmentShader; // blank sheet of paperfragmentShader = glCreateShader(GL\_FRAGMENT\_SHADER); // make a blank shader form//              V form       V how many strings  V where the code isglShaderSource(fragmentShader, 1, \&fragmentShaderSource, NULL); //what do we fill the form withglCompileShader(fragmentShader); // okay we've given you all the info, now sign the form][image10] |
| :---- |

**We pretty much carry out the same process but using GL\_FRAGMENT\_SHADER instead of GL\_VERTEX\_SHADER.**

**We use out vec4 FragColor to tell OpenGL what color we want to use. In the main function we set FragColor \= vec4(1.0f, 0.5f, 0.2f, 1.0f).  The ‘out’ is sending our variable to the next shader step.** 

**We could do the same thing in the *vertex shader.* Using ‘out’ to pass values to our *fragment shader* where we would catch it using ‘in’.** 

**This is because the *vertex shader* comes before the *fragment shader* in the pipeline.**

**The last thing we have to do with the shaders is link both as a Shader Program.**

| ![Image with code:unsigned int shaderProgram; // blank sheetshaderProgram = glCreateProgram(); // make it a shader program form//give the shader program the vertex and fragment shader forms// link them together to make a full shader program from the vertex and fragment shader formsglAttachShader(shaderProgram, vertexShader);glAttachShader(shaderProgram, fragmentShader);glLinkProgram(shaderProgram);// dont need these anymore since we have the shader program.glDeleteShader(vertexShader);glDeleteShader(fragmentShader);][image11] |
| :---- |

**Here we just attach our two shaders to our created shaderProgram, and then we link them. Afterwards we no longer need the vertexShader or fragmentShader as we have the final shader program, so they are deleted.**

---

**Part 7: Vertex Array Object**

**The Vertex Array Object is basically a save state for our VBO. When we bind it, it will save what VBO is currently bound and the vertex attributes that are used to interpret the VBO data into whatever shader we use.**

| \\![Image with code:unsigned int VAO; // blank sheet of paperglGenVertexArrays(1, \&VAO); // make a folder out of this paper glBindVertexArray(VAO); // okay now were gonna use this folder to put stuff in//this remembers whatever VBO is currently bound and vertexAtrribPointer settings.glBindBuffer(GL\_ARRAY\_BUFFER, VBO); // binding the VBO, this "puts it in the folder"//(technically it was already bound so we didnt have to bind it again)//how should our shaders interpret the VBO?//doing this while VAO is bound also "puts it in the folder"glVertexAttribPointer(&#9;0, // location = 0 in the vertex shader, so basically where in the shader the data goes&#9;3, // how many values for each vertex for this location? (x,y,z) so 3&#9;GL\_FLOAT, // what type of data is it? floats&#9;GL\_FALSE, // do we want the data to be normalized? no    3 \* sizeof(float), // total size of data for ALL locations.    nullptr); // where does the data start, glEnableVertexAttribArray(0); // enabling location 0glBindVertexArray(0);][image12] |
| :---- |

**glVertexAttribPointer(...) sets a vertex attribute.**  
**The first parameter is used to select what location we are going to be passing data into. Remember the location in the vertex shader? It was set to 0, so with this we are telling it to use location 0\.**

**The second parameter is used to tell it how many adjacent floats we are going to give this location. Since in the vertex code we are wanting a vec3, we give it 3 floats.**

**The third just tells it what type it is.**

**The fourth is normalization.**

**The fifth is the TOTAL size of ALL locations we are going to use with the VBO. Since we only have *one* location (location 0\) it is set to our 3 floats’ size.** 

**But if we had *two* locations (location 0 and location 1\) and in location 1 we wanted to save our texture coordinates (texture coordinates are 2 floats). Our fifth parameter would be 5 \* sizeof(float). 3 floats for the vertex, and 2 for the texture coords.**

	**glEnableVertexAttribArray(0)**   
**makes it so OpenGL will actually look for data for location 0**

**glBindVertexArray(0)**  
**Now we just unbind our VAO, we’ve set everything up inside it and now we unbind it so we don’t have it overwritten.**

---

**Part 8: Drawing the triangle**

	**Now it’s time to draw our triangle.**

| \\![Image with code://while the window shouldnt close, keep doing this loop.while (\!glfwWindowShouldClose(window)){&#9;glClearColor(0.2f, 0.3f, 0.3f, 1.0f); // set color buffer bit&#9;glClear(GL\_COLOR\_BUFFER\_BIT); // clear the screen with the color we just set    glBindVertexArray(VAO); // take out our folder with our VBO and attribute settings    glUseProgram(shaderProgram); // use the shader program we made    glDrawArrays(GL\_TRIANGLES, 0, 3);     //interpret data as triangles, vertices starting at 0 and going to 3                        //swap our buffers to update    glfwSwapBuffers(window);    glfwPollEvents();}][image13] |
| :---- |

**The first two functions just set a color to clear the screen with, and then use that color to wipe away all the previous rendering so we don’t have any “residue” from previous draws.**

**Inside our loop we bind our VAO which loads the state we have saved in it. Then we just tell it what shader program we want to use with our data.**

**After that we tell it we want it to draw our data as triangles, since we only have 3 vertices we tell it from 0 to 3\.**

**All that’s left is to run the program.**

| \\![Image of the final triangle that will be drawn.][image14] |
| :---- |
